LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD has a simple global scope consisting only of variable assignments. No `pkgver()` function is defined, so no code executes beyond setting variables when the file is sourced. The `source` array references only standard HTTPS URLs from the project's own GitHub repository (raw.githubusercontent.com and github.com). The checksums are provided and non-SKIP, meaning source integrity is verified. No dangerous constructs (eval, command substitution, obfuscation, or unexpected network destinations) are present. The commands `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` will safely download the declared sources and parse the PKGBUILD without executing any malicious code.
</details>
<summary>Standard PKGBUILD with no dangerous global code or sources.</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
This PKGBUILD follows standard AUR packaging practices for a pre-built binary application. It downloads the upstream binary tarball and documentation from the official GitHub repository (`github.com/johnsideserf/siggy`), with pinned checksums for the x86_64 release. The `package()` function only installs the binary, README, and license into the appropriate system directories. There is no obfuscated code, no unexpected network requests, no execution of untrusted code beyond the package's own binary, and no attempts to exfiltrate data or modify system files outside the package's scope.

Note: The `source_aarch64` array is defined without corresponding `sha256sums_aarch64`, but this does not affect the x86_64 build and is a minor hygiene issue rather than a security threat. No malicious behavior is present.
</details>
<summary>Standard AUR PKGBUILD; no malicious behavior detected.</summary>

It specifies that the package "siggy-bin" should be tracked on GitHub, using the repository `johnsideserf/siggy`, checking the latest release with a version prefix of "v". This is a standard, benign configuration that does not perform any actions itself. There is no code execution, no network requests within this file (it only defines parameters for nvchecker to later use), and no obfuscation or malicious intent. The file is safe.
</details>

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines variables and arrays for use in the package build process. During `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo`, only the global scope and `pkgver()` function are evaluated. This file contains no `pkgver()` function, so only variable assignments are executed. All `source` URLs are constructed using trusted, static values pointing to the declared GitHub repository (`https://github.com/johnsideserf/siggy`), with no dynamic command substitution or external calls. The sources consist of the prebuilt binary tarball for x86_64 (and aarch64, though not used on x86_64), along with README and LICENSE files fetched from the corresponding tag. Checksums are provided for the included sources, and no code is executed during sourcing. There is no evidence of data exfiltration, remote code execution, or dangerous operations in the top-level scope.
</details>
<summary>
No active malicious code in PKGBUILD scope.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No active malicious code in PKGBUILD scope.[/]

LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD fetches a pre-built binary release of siggy from the project's official GitHub repository (https://github.com/johnsideserf/siggy) for x86_64 and aarch64 architectures. The source URLs point to release binaries and static files (README and LICENSE) from a tagged version, which is a standard and expected practice. All sources have corresponding non-SKIP sha256sums, ensuring integrity. The package installs the binary to /usr/bin, and documentation/licenses to standard locations under /usr/share. There are no network operations, file modifications, or system calls beyond standard installation procedures. The dependencies (glibc, libgcc) are minimal and typical for a statically linked or partially linked binary. No obfuscated code, dynamic downloads, or suspicious commands (e.g., eval, curl|bash) are present. The script strictly follows AUR packaging best practices.
</details>
<summary>
Standard binary package from upstream GitHub release</summary>
</security_assessment>

LLM auditresponse for .nvchecker.toml:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided `.nvchecker.toml` file is a configuration file for nvchecker, a tool used to monitor upstream project versions, commonly in AUR packaging workflows. It defines a single source for the `siggy-bin` package from GitHub: `johnsideserf/siggy`, using the latest release and expecting a version tag prefix "v". This configuration is standard and benign.

There are no executable scripts, network requests, or file operations in this file. It does not perform downloads, execute external code, or exfiltrate data. The use of `use_latest_release = true` is a typical configuration choice for AUR packages that track upstream releases and does not introduce a supply-chain risk, as it only informs version checking.

The file contains no obfuscated content, dangerous commands, or unexpected behavior. It solely configures a version-tracking tool and poses no security threat.
</details>
<summary>
Configuration file for version tracking; no malicious content
</summary>
</security_assessment>

[1/2] Reviewing README-1.9.1.md...
+ Reviewed .nvchecker.toml. Status: SAFE -- Configuration file for version tracking; no malicious content
LLM auditresponse for README-1.9.1.md:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The provided file is a README-1.9.1.md, which serves as documentation for the siggy-bin package. It describes the functionality, installation methods, configuration options, and usage of siggy, a terminal-based Signal messenger client. The content includes standard installation instructions such as using Homebrew, downloading pre-built binaries via curl or PowerShell, installing from crates.io, and building from source. All described network operations (e.g., downloading install.sh or install.ps1) are directed to the official GitHub repository (github.com/johnsideserf/siggy), which aligns with expected packaging and distribution practices.

There is no executable code in the file, and all commands shown are intended to be run manually by the user. The installation scripts referenced (install.sh, install.ps1) are not included in this file, so they cannot be analyzed here—but their invocation via curl|bash or iex is common for developer tools and not inherently malicious when sourcing from verified project repositories. The documentation also clearly states prerequisites such as having signal-cli available, which indicates expected upstream dependencies. There are no obfuscated scripts, suspicious network calls, or unauthorized system modifications described or implied.

The file contains only explanatory text, badges linking to public CI and release status, and standard usage examples. These are consistent with normal open-source project documentation. Nothing in this file constitutes injected malicious code or supply-chain attack vectors. Upstream functionality such as message syncing, notifications, and contact resolution are within the expected behavior of a messaging client.
</details>
<summary>Documentation-only file, no malicious content</summary>
</security_assessment>
